description: Maps error patterns to common mistakes and fix suggestions from calor-language-skills.md

patterns.title: Null-Check Ordering (CRITICAL)
patterns.description: Checking array/string length without null check first causes NullReferenceException instead of ContractViolationException.
patterns.wrongExample: §Q (> (len arr) 0)            // Crashes if arr is null!
patterns.correctExample:
§Q (!= arr null)              // First: reject null
§Q (> (len arr) 0)            // Then: check length (safe now)
patterns.explanation: Contracts are evaluated in order. If you call (len arr) when arr is null, you get a crash instead of a proper contract violation. Always guard reference types with (!= x null) before accessing their properties.

patterns.title: String Operation Syntax
patterns.description: Calor uses specific syntax for string operations. Don't invent C#-style methods.
patterns.wrongExample:
§R (+ type ":" (ToString id))
§R §CONCAT "TOKEN-" §TOSTR userId
patterns.explanation: Use (str x) to convert to string, (concat ...) to join strings, (fmt "...") for formatting, (len s) for string length.

patterns.title: indexof Argument Count
patterns.description: The indexof function only takes 2 arguments (string and search term). Use substr for offset searches.
patterns.wrongExample: §B{next} (indexof s "@" (+ first 1))  // 3 args not supported
patterns.explanation: indexof only takes 2 arguments. To search from an offset, use substr to create a substring first, then search within it.

patterns.title: Variable Redeclaration
patterns.description: Declare a variable once with §B. Use §B{~name} for a mutable binding, then reassign it later with §ASSIGN name value — do not declare the same name twice.
patterns.wrongExample:
§B{k} (% rng n)    // First use: declares k
§B{k} (+ k 1)      // ERROR - 'k' already defined
patterns.correctExample:
§B{~k} (% rng n)   // Declare k once (mutable)
§ASSIGN k (+ k 1)  // Reassign k — no re-declaration
patterns.explanation: §B declares a NEW binding, so using it twice for the same name is a redeclaration. Declare once with §B{~name} for a value that changes, then update it with §ASSIGN name value (a second §B{~name} would re-declare, not update).

patterns.title: Character Literal Syntax
patterns.description: Calor does not support single-quoted character literals. Use character predicates or char-code instead.
patterns.wrongExample: §IF{if1} (== c '0')   // ERROR: Single quotes not supported
patterns.correctExample:
§IF{if1} (is-digit c)                    // Check if digit
§IF{if2} (== (char-code c) 45)           // Check if c == '-' (code 45)
§B{equalChar} (char-from-code 61)        // '=' character (code 61)
§B{equalChar} (char-at "=" 0)             // '=' from string
patterns.explanation: Use (is-digit c) for digit checks, (char-code c) for numeric comparison, (char-from-code n) for constants, or (char-at "x" 0) to extract from a string.

patterns.title: Array Type Syntax
patterns.description: Array type brackets go BEFORE the type name in Calor, not after.
patterns.wrongExample: §F{f1:UseItems:pub} (i32[]:items) -> i32     // ERROR - wrong array type syntax
patterns.explanation: In Calor, array types use [T] syntax with brackets before the type name: [i32], [str], [f64].

patterns.title: Array Element Access
patterns.description: Use §IDX for array element access. Cannot nest §IDX inside Lisp expressions.
patterns.wrongExample:
§B{x} (get arr i)      // ERROR - 'get' doesn't exist
§B{x} arr[i]           // ERROR - no C# indexer
§B{~sum} (+ sum §IDX data i)  // ERROR - tag inside Lisp expr
patterns.correctExample:
§B{x} §IDX arr i       // arr[i]
§SETIDX{arr} i value   // arr[i] = value

// For use in expressions, extract to binding first:
§B{val} §IDX data i
§B{~sum} (+ sum val)
patterns.explanation: Use §IDX for reading (no braces), §SETIDX{arr} for writing. §IDX cannot be nested inside Lisp expressions — extract to a binding first.

patterns.title: String Comparison
patterns.description: Relational operators (< >) don't work on strings. Use (equals) for equality.
patterns.wrongExample: §IF{if1} (< a b) → §R (- 0 1)   // ERROR - can't compare strings with <
patterns.correctExample:
§IF{if1} (equals a b) → §R 0
// For lexicographic comparison, compare character by character using (char-at) and (char-code).
patterns.explanation: String types cannot use < or > operators. Use (equals a b) for equality. For ordering, compare character codes.

patterns.title: Missing Built-in Operators
patterns.description: abs, max, min, sqrt, pow are not built-in. Use IF branches instead.
patterns.wrongExample:
§R (abs x)     // ERROR - abs doesn't exist
§R (max a b)   // ERROR - max doesn't exist
patterns.correctExample:
§IF{if1} (< x 0) → §R (- 0 x)
§R x

§IF{if2} (> a b) → §R a
§R b
patterns.explanation: Calor doesn't have abs, max, min, sqrt, pow as built-in functions. Use §IF with a single return or an indented block to implement branch logic.

patterns.title: Arrow Syntax Limitations
patterns.description: Arrow syntax (→) is for single statements only. Use block syntax for multiple statements.
patterns.wrongExample: §IF{if1} (condition) → §B{x} 1 → §R x   // ERROR
patterns.explanation: The arrow (→) can only be followed by a single statement. For multiple statements, put an indented block under the §IF opener; the block ends at dedent.

patterns.title: Target-Typed New Placeholder
patterns.description: The converter generates §NEW{object} when it can't resolve the target type of a C# target-typed 'new()'. Replace 'object' with the actual type.
patterns.wrongExample: §TH §NEW{object} §A "Invalid input" §/NEW   // 'object' is a placeholder
patterns.explanation: Look at the context — throw statements usually need an Exception type, assignments need the variable's declared type, method returns need the return type. Replace §NEW{object} with the correct concrete type.

patterns.title: Integer Literal Overflow
patterns.description: Large integer literals may overflow i32 range. Calor now supports i64 natively — values outside int range are handled automatically.
patterns.wrongExample: §C{Method} §A -1486618624   // This was 1000000000000 truncated to i32!
patterns.explanation: If you see suspiciously negative numbers where large positive values are expected, the original C# likely used long/Int64 literals. Re-convert the file to pick up the i64 fix.

patterns.title: Generic Type Parameters in Expressions
patterns.description: Generic type arguments like <T> in call expressions are not yet fully supported in expression contexts.
patterns.wrongExample: §C{EnumCache<T>.GetInfo} §/C   // Parser rejects <T> in expressions
patterns.correctExample: §B{~info} §C{EnumCache.GetInfo} §/C   // Use without type param, let C# infer
patterns.explanation: When converting generic method calls, remove the type arguments from the call expression if C# type inference can resolve them. For explicit type args, use a §CSHARP interop block.

patterns.title: Legacy IF Closing Tag
patterns.description: Current Calor IF blocks are indentation-delimited. Do not add legacy IF closing tags or try to match IF IDs.
patterns.wrongExample:
§IF{if016} (condition)
  §R 1
§/I{if017}   // ERROR: legacy structural closing tag
patterns.explanation: Indent the IF body under §IF. The block ends at dedent, so there is no IF closing tag or closing ID to match.

patterns.title: Invalid Lisp Expression
patterns.description: The parser encountered an unexpected token inside a Lisp-style expression (parenthesized prefix notation). Common causes: postfix nullable type syntax (T?), unsupported operator, or malformed nested expression.
patterns.wrongExample:
(cast T? value)          // Use prefix ?T for option/nullable types
(+ a §IDX arr i)         // Can't nest § tags in Lisp expressions
patterns.correctExample:
(cast ?T value)          // Prefix option/nullable type
§B{_tmp} §IDX arr i      // Extract to binding first
(+ a _tmp)
patterns.explanation: Lisp expressions use prefix notation: (operator arg1 arg2). Types must use Calor type forms such as ?T for option/nullable values. Section markers (§) cannot appear inside parenthesized expressions.

patterns.title: Legacy Block Closing ID
patterns.description: Current Calor blocks are indentation-delimited; legacy closing-tag IDs for IF, LOOP, TRY, or MATCH are obsolete. Similar parser errors usually mean an unwanted closing tag or bad indentation.
patterns.wrongExample:
§L{loop1:i:0:n:1}
  §IF{if1} (condition)
    §R i
§/L{loop2}   // ERROR: legacy structural closing tag
patterns.explanation: Remove legacy closing tags and indent nested bodies. Blocks end at the next dedent; there are no structural IDs to match.

patterns.title: Expected Keyword
patterns.description: The parser expected a Calor section marker such as §M or §F but found something else. This usually means obsolete keyword syntax or a malformed declaration.
patterns.wrongExample:
module MyApp         // Obsolete keyword syntax
  function Add(a b)    // 'function' is not a Calor marker
patterns.explanation: Use current Calor section markers: §M{id:Name} for modules and §F{id:name:vis} (type:name, ...) -> retType for functions. Do not use C# or legacy keyword forms.

patterns.title: Expected Indented Block
patterns.description: A legacy diagnostic may mention closing tags, but current Calor blocks are indentation-delimited. This usually means a block body is missing or incorrectly indented.
patterns.wrongExample:
§IF{if1} (> x 0)
§R x   // ERROR: body must be indented under §IF
patterns.explanation: Do not add structural closing tags. Indent the block body under the opener; the block ends when indentation returns to the parent level.

patterns.title: Invalid Modifier
patterns.description: A modifier keyword was used in an invalid position or an unrecognized modifier was applied to a declaration.
patterns.wrongExample:
static §F{f1:Helper:pub} () -> i32   // C# modifier before Calor marker
§F{f2:Bad:public} () -> i32           // Use pub/priv, not public/private
patterns.explanation: Calor stores visibility and modifiers inside declaration markers (for example pub or priv). C# modifier keywords like public, private, protected, and static are not written before §F.

patterns.title: Type Parameter Not Found
patterns.description: A reference to a generic type parameter (like T, K, V) was used but the parameter is not declared in the current scope.
patterns.wrongExample: §F{f1:Identity:pub} (T:x) -> T      // T not declared as type param
patterns.explanation: Generic type parameters must be declared in the function or type name field using angle brackets. Ensure the type parameter name matches exactly (case-sensitive).

patterns.title: Invalid Reference
patterns.description: A reference to a variable, function, or type is invalid in the current context. This may be a scope issue or an attempt to use a binding before it is defined.
patterns.wrongExample: §R (+ a b)      // 'a' or 'b' not accessible in this scope
patterns.correctExample:
§B{a} 1
§B{b} 2
§R (+ a b)      // Both bindings are in scope
patterns.explanation: Ensure the referenced name is defined and accessible in the current scope. Bindings (§B) are only visible after their declaration point and within the same block.

patterns.title: Missing Extension Self Parameter
patterns.description: An extension function is missing the required 'self' first parameter. Extension functions in Calor must declare the receiver type as the first parameter.
patterns.wrongExample: §F{f1:ToUpper:pub} () -> str     // Missing self parameter
patterns.explanation: Extension functions require a 'self' parameter as the first argument to specify the type being extended. This is equivalent to the 'this' parameter in C# extension methods.

patterns.title: Auto-Fixable: NEW{object} Placeholder
patterns.description: The converter generated §NEW{object} where a concrete type is needed. Use calor_fix with error code 'new_object' to auto-replace from context (binding type, field type, throw → Exception, return type).
patterns.wrongExample:
§F{f1:Make:pub} () -> List<i32>
  §R §NEW{object}()
patterns.explanation: The calor_fix tool infers the correct type from surrounding context: binding values, field/property types, throw statements (→ Exception), or function/method return types.

patterns.title: Auto-Fixable: Arrow Body with Multiple Statements
patterns.description: Arrow syntax (→) only supports a single statement. Use calor_fix with error code 'arrow_multi_statement' to convert to block form automatically.
patterns.wrongExample: §IF{if1} (condition) → §B{x} 1 §R x
patterns.explanation: The calor_fix tool detects arrow bodies with multiple §-tagged statements and rewrites them as indented block bodies.

patterns.title: Auto-Fixable: Duplicate Declaration IDs
patterns.description: Duplicate declaration IDs (e.g., two §F{f1:...}) cause compilation errors. Use calor_fix with error code 'id_conflicts' to renumber duplicates automatically.
patterns.wrongExample:
§F{f1:Foo:pub} () -> void
  §R
§F{f1:Bar:pub} () -> void
  §R
patterns.explanation: The calor_fix tool scans for duplicate IDs and renumbers the second and subsequent occurrences. Blocks are indentation-delimited, so only declaration IDs need updating.
